ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers fruit juice food products broadly; no exact code for packaged fruit juice with pulp exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fruit juices broadly; no specific sub-code for pulp-containing packaged variants. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged fruit juice with pulp as a food product in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion is one 250 ml carton or cup, equivalent to approximately 250 g, as commonly sold in Singapore convenience stores and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit juice with pulp (packaged, any flavour) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record for 'Fruit juice with pulp (packaged, any flavour)', cr_id D03040014, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the HPB source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — fruit juice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 assigned based on University of Sydney GI Database values for packaged fruit juices with pulp. Orange juice (unsweetened, packaged) has a published GI of approximately 50–57 in the Sydney database; GI 55 is used as a central analogous estimate for a generic packaged fruit juice with pulp. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of exactly 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category. gi_category is therefore set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 18.33 × 2.5) / 100 = 25.2 for a 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.41 g/100 g) in a liquid beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention, per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (17 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 10.41 g/100 g sugar load exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold and independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of its low sodium content — choose whole fruit or water instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 10.41 g/100 g in liquid form with only 1.25 g/100 g dietary fibre causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes; GI of 55 and glycemic load of 25.2 per 250 ml serving are clinically significant for glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid packaged fruit juice with pulp; the liquid sugar matrix and modest fibre content (1.25 g/100 g) are insufficient to blunt glucose absorption, and the ADA recommends replacing juice with whole fruit to benefit from intact fibre and slower glucose release.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (10.41 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage profile is associated with elevated triglycerides, increased LDL particle density, and cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines; fat is 0 g/100 g and sodium is low at 17 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage due to its high sugar load (10.41 g/100 g), which is linked to hypertriglyceridaemia and adverse lipid profiles; the absence of fat and low sodium are positive factors but do not offset the sugar-driven cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged fruit juice with pulp is a ready-to-drink commercial beverage containing fruit juice and suspended fruit pulp pieces, available in a variety of flavours including orange, mango, guava, and mixed tropical fruit. It is sold in cartons, bottles, or pouches and is widely available in Singapore supermarkets, convenience stores, and food courts. The beverage has a sweet, fruity taste with a slightly thick texture due to the presence of fruit pulp, which contributes a small amount of dietary fibre (1.25 g per 100 g). Per 100 g, it provides 63 kcal, 18.33 g carbohydrate, 10.41 g sugar, 0.83 g protein, 0 g fat, 1.25 g dietary fibre, and 17 mg sodium. The iron content is 1.66 mg/100 g and calcium is 1.0 mg/100 g. Despite containing fruit pulp, the fibre content is insufficient to meaningfully slow glucose absorption in liquid form. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, at the upper boundary of the low GI range) with a glycemic load of approximately 25.2 per 250 ml serving, making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 10.41 g/100 g sugar content, and is considered unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Whole fruit is consistently recommended as a superior alternative due to its intact fibre matrix and lower sugar density per serving.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03040014) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for packaged fruit juices, documented as such.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03040014); this is naturally zero as fruit juice is an inherently fat-free beverage product. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by formulation as the product contains no fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this record. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food record (HPB Singapore Foodo, cr_id D03040014, accessed 2026-04-16). GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for packaged orange juice and tropical fruit juices; the input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.367, which is not an approved source — the GI value of 55 has been retained as it aligns with the Sydney database analogous range, but the source has been updated to the approved University of Sydney GI Database reference. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category; gi_category is therefore set to 'low', correcting the previous iteration's 'medium' assignment. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as (55 × 18.33 g carbohydrate × 2.5 serving size factor) / 100 = 25.2 for a standard 250 g serving. This food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to sugar content of 10.41 g/100 g, which exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold used in clinical dietary guidelines.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input, added multilingual names, ontology, health context, GI from approved source.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Verified gi_category consistency.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55 per standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes and llm description to reflect corrected category. Updated notes to document the correction rationale.
